raw_case:
Malignant granular cell tumour of floor of mouth (non-neural in origin) – A rare case report and review of literature
Five cases of non-neuronal granular cell tumours of the oral cavity are documented in the literature. Additionally, one case of a non-neuronal granular cell tumour with features of malignancy was described. A malignant granular cell tumour is a rare neoplasm and counterpart of a benign granular cell tumour. The cell of origin of the granular cell tumour was reported from the Schwann cell. Some granular cells originated from non-neural components and were negative for immunohistochemistry S100. Immunohistochemistry is required to confirm further and categorize ulcero-proliferative and erythematous polypoidal oral cavity lesions. These lesions can mimic squamous cell carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma and pyogenic granuloma in morphology. We are presenting a rare case of malignant granular cell tumour of non-neuronal origin on the floor of the mouth. To our knowledge, it is the first case of a malignant non-neuronal granular cell tumour.

Background

A malignant granular cell tumour is a rare neoplasm and counterpart of a benign granular cell tumour.
1
 The cell of origin of the granular cell tumour was reported from the Schwann cell.
2
 But some granular cells were originated from non-neural components and negative for IHC (immunohistochemistry) S100.
3
 Initially, these tumours (non-neuronal/negative for S-100 were called ‘Primitive polypoid granular cell tumours’.
4
Granular cell (GC) tumours can be present anywhere in the body, but 50% are present in the head and neck region, with the most common sites being the tongue (80%) in the oral cavity, skin and soft tissue.2,5,6 The classical presentation of GC tumour is solitary, painless and 3–4 cm in size in middle-aged females.
5
 The literature reported five cases of non-neuronal (S-100 negative) granular cell tumours in the oral cavity.7–10 Malignant GC tumour is a rare entity, reported in approximately 1%–2% of cases and having a bad prognosis.
11
 We are writing about the sixth case of a non-neuronal granular cell tumour with its malignant counterpart. The indexed study aimed to present a rare case of malignant granular cell tumour of non-neuronal origin on the floor of the mouth.

Case report

A 57-year-old male presented with ulcero-proliferative growth on the floor of the mouth for three years and came in ENT OPD. A biopsy was taken with patients’ written informed consent and sent to the histopathology department. Grossly, we received multiple soft tissue pieces, altogether measuring 1.8 × 1.2 × 0.5. A microscopic examination revealed a fragmented biopsy; most fragments showed necrosis, and a few viable fragments showed a tumour arranged in solid sheets and focal short fascicles. The tumour cells are large polygonal with round-to-oval nuclei, vesicular nuclear chromatin, mild nuclear pleomorphism and prominent nucleoli with moderate-to-abundant amounts of fine granular cytoplasm (Figure 1(a) and (b)). Few areas showed spindle cell differentiation with elongated hyperchromatic nuclei, irregular membranes, moderate nuclear pleomorphism and a scant-to-moderate amount of pale cytoplasm (Figure 1(c)). In addition, large areas of necrosis (Figure 1(d)), a few atypical (0–4)/10 HPF mitosis and apoptotic bodies were also noted. On histomorphology, the diagnosis suggested a malignant granular cell tumour. On IHC examination, the tumour cells were positive for vimentin (strongly, diffuse membranous and cytoplasmic), NSE (nonspecific enolase, diffuse cytoplasmic) and CD10 (cytoplasmic granular) (Figure 2(a)–(c)). The tumour cells were negative for S100 (Figure 2(d)), P40, CK5/6, and Pan-CK. The diagnosis confirmed a malignant granular cell tumour of non-neuronal origin.

Discussion

In this case, we found an abnormal location of granular cell tumour on the floor of the mouth and a lack of expression of S-100 on IHC. There were five cases of non-neuronal granular cell tumours in the literature compared with our case in Tables 1 and 2 for clinical, histomorphology and IHC.7–10Histomorphologically, that tumour showed high cellularity, spindle cell differentiation, hyperchromasia, moderate nuclear pleomorphism, vesicular nuclear chromatin, prominent nucleoli and a large area of necrosis, and atypical mitotic figures (0–4/10 HPF) favouring malignant granular cell tumour; similar histomorphology has been described in one study.
12
 Our case findings corroborated this study, which was reported as a malignant granular cell tumour like the Fanburg-Smith criteria that assessed the following six histologic criteria: necrosis, spindling, vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli, increased mitotic activity (>2 mitoses/10 high-power fields at 200× magnification), high nuclear to cytoplasmic (N:C) ratio and pleomorphism.
13
 Most of the granular cell tumours originate from Schwann cells of Nevers.
2
 On IHC, these GC tumours were positive for S-100 and NSE.
2
 But some studies demonstrated their origin from a non-neuronal component. Cohen et al. reported a non-neural granular cell tumour (NNGCT) ( also known as primitive polypoid granular cell tumour) different from a conventional GC tumour by expressing more nuclear atypia, relatively high mitosis (0–10/10 HPF) and negative for S-100 IHC.
13
 Conventional GC tumour was positive for vimentin, S-100, NSE, CD68, CD56, CD57, calretinin and inhibin.
2
 That was different from NNGCT, which was positive for vimentin, NSE, CD68 and CD10, and negative for S-100, with similar positivity of IHC found in our case.13,14 Some studies reported NNGCT-associated fusion of the ALK gene and positive for Anaplastic Lymphoma Kinase (ALK) on IHC.
3
 Due to our department’s lack of ALK IHC, we could not do this in this case. The patients of NNGCT were managed with complete surgical excision along with chemotherapy and radiotherapy.1,7,10 Index case was lost to follow-up after complete surgery.There were a few case series of cutaneous granular cell tumours and polypoidal features. It was negative for S-100 IHC, first described by LeBoit et al. in 1991.4,3,15 Differential diagnoses of malignant granular cell tumour based on histomorphology are alveolar soft parts sarcoma, angiosarcoma, atypical fibroxanthoma, congenital granular cell epulis, dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, epithelioid histiocytoma, fibroxanthoma, granular cell dermatofibroma, hibernoma, leiomyosarcoma, malignant fibrous histiocytoma, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour, melanoma, neurofibroma, granular cell tumour, reactive granular cell change, reticulohistiocytoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, schwannoma, squamous cell carcinoma, trichoblastoma and xanthoma.
16
 Long-standing polypoidal growth with minimum symptoms indicated NNGCT. Until now, six cases of non-neuronal granular cell tumours of the oral cavity have been described; our case is the first case of malignant granular cell tumour.

Limitations

We did not follow up our patient.

Conclusion

Through this case, we presented a rare case of a non-neuronal malignant granular cell tumour at an unusual location (floor of mouth) and confirmed with IHC. To our knowledge, it is the first case of a malignant non-neuronal granular cell tumour.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 57-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Ulcero-proliferative growth on the floor of the mouth for three years.
- **History of Present Illness:** Long-standing polypoidal growth for three years with minimal symptoms.
- **Past Medical History:** None indicated.
- **Personal History:** None indicated.
- **Family History:** None indicated.
- **Physical Examination:** Findings not mentioned.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Histopathology: Tumor arranged in solid sheets and fascicles, large polygonal and spindle cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, pleomorphism, granular cytoplasm, necrosis, atypical mitosis (0–4/10 HPF), and apoptotic bodies.
  - Immunohistochemistry (IHC): Positive for vimentin, NSE (nonspecific enolase), and CD10; negative for S100, P40, CK5/6, and Pan-CK.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - The patient's ulcero-proliferative lesion in the oral cavity with histomorphological evidence raised a broad spectrum of potential diagnoses, including squamous cell carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, pyogenic granuloma, and other neoplasms of epithelial, mesenchymal, or neural origin.

2. **Stepwise Evaluation:**
   - **Squamous Cell Carcinoma:** Excluded due to lack of squamous differentiation on histology and negative IHC markers (P40, CK5/6, Pan-CK).
   - **Malignant Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor or Neural Tumors:** Excluded based on S100 negativity, typically positive in Schwann cell origin lesions.
   - **Granular Cell Tumor (Conventional):** Initially considered due to granular cell histomorphology; however, exclusion based on S100 negativity.
   - **Non-Neuronal Granular Cell Tumor (Primitive Polypoid Granular Cell Tumor):** Strongly considered due to lack of S100 staining and positivity for vimentin, NSE, and CD10, aligning with previously reported cases.
   - **Malignant Tumor Subtype:** Established by the presence of necrosis, pleomorphism, high N:C ratio, mitotic activity, and other malignant features consistent with Fanburg-Smith criteria.

3. **Confirmation:**
   - IHC and histomorphology confirmed the final diagnosis as a malignant granular cell tumor of non-neuronal origin.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient has a malignant granular cell tumor of non-neuronal origin on the floor of the mouth. This rare diagnosis was established based on histopathological features (necrosis, spindling, atypical mitosis, pleomorphism) and IHC results (positive for vimentin, NSE, CD10; negative for S100).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Malignant granular cell tumor of non-neuronal origin.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

First, I need to carefully analyze the given patient case to arrive at an accurate diagnosis. The patient is a 57-year-old male presenting with a three-year history of an ulcero-proliferative growth on the floor of the mouth. The growth is described as polypoidal with minimal symptoms. There's no mention of past medical history, personal history, or family history that might be relevant, so I'll focus on the physical examination findings and the histopathological report.

Starting with the physical examination, it's noted that there are findings, but they're not specified in the summary provided. This is a bit of a gap, as the appearance and characteristics of the growth could provide important clues. However, I'll have to rely on the histopathology and immunohistochemistry results for diagnosis.

Looking at the histopathology description:

- Tumor arranged in solid sheets and fascicles

- Cells are large, polygonal, and spindle-shaped with vesicular nuclei

- Prominent nucleoli

- Pleomorphism

- Granular cytoplasm

- Necrosis

- Atypical mitosis (0–4 per 10 high-power fields)

- Apoptotic bodies

These features suggest a malignant neoplasm, given the presence of atypical cells, mitoses, necrosis, and pleomorphism. The arrangement in sheets and fascicles could indicate a sarcomatous or spindle cell carcinoma.

Now, considering the immunohistochemistry results:

- Positive for vimentin, NSE (nonspecific enolase), and CD10

- Negative for S100, P40, CK5/6, and Pan-CK

Vimentin is a mesenchymal marker, so its positivity suggests a mesenchymal or neural crest origin. NSE is often associated with neuroendocrine tumors, and CD10 is seen in various neoplasms, including some sarcomas and certain carcinomas.

The negative markers are also important:

- S100 is typically expressed in neural and melanocytic tumors; its negativity makes those less likely.

- P40 and CK5/6 are markers often used in squamous cell carcinomas, and pan-CK is a broad epithelial marker; their negativity suggests that this is not a typical squamous cell carcinoma or other epithelial carcinoma.

Given the location (floor of the mouth), I should consider oral cavity malignancies. Common tumors in this area include squamous cell carcinomas, but the IHC negativity for epithelial markers makes that less likely. Other possibilities include sarcomas, given the mesenchymal markers, or potentially neuroendocrine tumors, given the NSE positivity.

Let me consider salivary gland neoplasms, as the floor of the mouth contains minor salivary glands. However, typical salivary gland tumors like adenoid cystic carcinoma, mucoepidermoid carcinoma, or pleomorphic adenoma usually have different histological features and IHC profiles.

Given the spindle cell and polygonal morphology, along with mesenchymal marker expression, I should consider malignant mesenchymal tumors, such as leiomyosarcoma or rhabdomyosarcoma. However, these are less common in the oral cavity, and their IHC profiles might not fully align with the markers provided.

Another consideration is a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST), which can express vimentin and occasionally NSE, but S100 is usually positive, which it isn't in this case.

The positivity for CD10 is interesting. CD10 is expressed in various neoplasms, including some sarcomas like synovial sarcoma, but synovial sarcoma typically expresses cytokeratins as well, which is negative here.

Perhaps I should consider a round cell malignant tumor that can occur in the head and neck region, such as Ewing's sarcoma or primitive neuroectodermal tumor (PNET). These tumors can be CD99 positive, but the IHC panel here doesn't include CD99. They also typically express other neural markers, which aren't positive here.

Another possibility is a clear cell sarcoma, which can occur in the head and neck and can express NSE and be negative for S100, but usually, clear cell sarcomas have more melanocytic differentiation markers, which are negative in this case.

Given the location and the histological features, I should also consider metastatic disease, but the patient has no known past medical history of cancer, and the growth has been present for three years with minimal symptoms, which makes a metastasis less likely.

Perhaps it's a unusual variant of a more common tumor, or a rare entity. The combination of vimentin and NSE positivity, along with CD10, in a spindle cell tumor is not straightforward.

Wait a minute, NSE can be expressed in some types of neural and neuroendocrine tumors, but also in some sarcomas. Maybe there's a neuroendocrine differentiation in a sarcoma?

Alternatively, could this be a poorly differentiated carcinoma with neuroendocrine features? However, the negativity for pan-CK makes that less likely, unless there'sCK negativeneuroendocrine tumor, which is rare.

Another thought: could this be a lymphoma or a malignant melanoma? Lymphomas can have spindle cell variants, but usually express lymphoid markers. Melanomas typically express S100, which is negative here.

Perhaps I need to consider a tumor with both epithelial and mesenchymal differentiation, like a carcinoma with spindle cell features, but the negativity for CK markers argues against that.

Wait, maybe it's a carcinosarcoma, which is a malignancy that demonstrates both carcinoma and sarcoma features. However, carcinosarcomas are rare, and typically express both epithelial and mesenchymal markers, but in this case, the epithelial markers are negative.

This is confusing. Maybe I need to reconsider the possibility of a sarcoma, given the mesenchymal marker expression and the spindle cell morphology.

Let's consider a leiomyosarcoma, which can express vimentin and occasionally NSE, but usually, leiomyosarcomas are smooth muscle tumors and might express smooth muscle actin or desmin, which aren't mentioned here.

Alternatively, a rhabdomyosarcoma could be considered, but that's more common in children and typically expresses myogenin or MyoD1, which aren't mentioned.

Another option is a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST), but as previously considered, the S100 negativity makes that less likely.

Perhaps it's a round cell meningioma, but those are usually intracranial and express epithelial markers.

Wait, the floor of the mouth is close to the nasal cavity and skull base; could it be a extension of a skull base tumor? But that seems unlikely given the long-standing nature and the IHC profile.

Another angle: could this be a odontogenic tumor, like an ameloblastoma or a malignant variant? However, ameloblastomas typically have epithelial features and express pan-CK.

This is tricky. Maybe it's a poorly differentiated sarcoma without specific lineage markers.

Alternatively, a metastatic tumor from an unknown primary, but again, the patient has no history of cancer, and it's been present for three years.

Perhaps it's a tumor with neuroendocrine differentiation, given the NSE positivity, but the lack of epithelial markers is perplexing.

Wait, maybe it's a small cell carcinoma, which can be neuroendocrine in nature and sometimes lack CK expression, but that's rare.

Alternatively, a peripheral neuroendocrine tumor, but those are extremely rare in the oral cavity.

Given the prolonged duration and minimal symptoms, maybe it's a low-grade malignancy or a tumor with mixed differentiation.

Alternatively, could this be a Langerhans cell histiocytosis, which can have variant morphologies, but typically expresses CD1a and other histiocytic markers, which aren't mentioned here.

This is quite challenging. Perhaps it's a tumor not commonly encountered in the oral cavity, or a tumor with unusual immunophenotype.

Maybe it's a malignant transformation of a benign lesion, like a fibroma or a neurofibroma, but that doesn't align well with the IHC markers.

Another possibility: a synovial sarcoma, which can express CD10 and have a spindle cell morphology, but typically also expresses cytokeratins in part of the tumor, which isn't the case here.

Perhaps I need to consider a tumor with neural crest origin, given the NSE positivity and the location, but S100 negativity is confusing.

Wait, could it be a clear cell sarcoma of soft parts, which is a type of malignant melanoma, but usually expresses melanocytic markers, which are negative here.

Alternatively, a epithelioid sarcoma, which can have spindle cell areas and express CD10, but typically also express epithelial markers, which are negative.

This is getting complicated. Maybe it's a tumor with nonspecific IHC markers, and the diagnosis needs to be based on a combination of histology and clinical presentation.

Given the floor of the mouth location and the three-year duration, it's probably not an aggressive tumor, but still, the histology suggests malignancy.

Perhaps it's a type of sarcoma not commonly seen in the oral cavity, or a tumor with mixed epithelial and mesenchymal features, but with loss of epithelial markers.

Alternatively, it could be a low-grade malignancy with minimal symptoms.

I think the best approach is to consider the most likely possibilities based on the IHC panel and histology, and then rank them in order of probability.

First possibility: a sarcoma, possibly a type of soft tissue sarcoma like leiomyosarcoma or fibrosarcoma, given the spindle cell morphology and vimentin expression.

Second possibility: a neuroendocrine tumor with spindle cell features, given the NSE positivity.

Third possibility: a round cell malignancy like Ewing's sarcoma or PNET, though the IHC profile isn't entirely matching.

Fourth possibility: a metastatic tumor from an unknown primary, possibly a neuroendocrine carcinoma.

However, without more specific markers or additional testing, it's challenging to narrow it down further.

Given the information provided, I think the most reasonable diagnosis is a soft tissue sarcoma, possibly of neural or neuroendocrine differentiation, given the NSE expression, but without definitive markers for a specific subtype.

Perhaps the pathologist is leaning towards a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor, despite the S100 negativity, or a neuroendocrine tumor with spindle cell features.

Alternatively, it could be a sarcoma with neuroendocrine differentiation, which is a rare entity.

In any case, further immunohistochemical staining or molecular testing might be needed to confirm the diagnosis and guide management.

But based on the current data, I would diagnose this as a spindle cell sarcoma with neuroendocrine features, given the histology and IHC findings.

### Answer:

Spindle cell sarcoma with neuroendocrine features

<Step 2> Squamous cell carcinoma was excluded due to lack of squamous differentiation on histology and negative IHC markers (P40, CK5/6, Pan-CK).
<Step 3> Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor or neural tumors were excluded based on S100 negativity, typical in Schwann cell origin lesions.
<Step 4> Granular cell tumor (conventional) was initially considered due to granular cell histomorphology but excluded based on S100 negativity.

<Step 5> Non-neuronal granular cell tumor (primitive polypoid granular cell tumor) was strongly considered due to lack of S100 staining and positivity for vimentin, NSE, and CD10, aligning with previous reports.
<Step 6> The presence of necrosis, pleomorphism, high N:C ratio, mitotic activity, and other malignant features consistent with Fanburg-Smith criteria established the malignant tumor subtype.
<Step 7> IHC and histomorphology confirmed the final diagnosis as a malignant granular cell tumor of non-neuronal origin.

<Step 8> The patient has a malignant granular cell tumor of non-neuronal origin on the floor of the mouth, established based on histopathological features (necrosis, spindling, atypical mitosis, pleomorphism) and IHC results (positive for vimentin, NSE, CD10; negative for S100).
